title: St. Cyril of Alexandria (Departure) & St. Methodius of Constantinople — St. Jerome (Monthly) & St. Vitus the Child-Martyr
narrative:
The Departure of St. Cyril the Great Archbishop of Alexandria the Pillar of Faith, St. Methodius the Patriarch of Constantinople the Confessor, the Monthly Commemoration of St. Jerome of Bethlehem the Doctor of Scripture, and St. Vitus the Child-Martyr

On the fourteenth of Sene, the holy Church commemorates the departure — the blessed death and entry into glory — of the holy and great Father Cyril, Archbishop of Alexandria, Pillar of Faith, Seal of the Fathers, the man who received from the Council of Ephesus in four hundred and thirty-one the triumph for which he had laboured and suffered and whom the tradition honours not only in his monthly commemoration but on this day of his actual departure as the one who completed what Athanasius had begun: Athanasius had defined the full divinity of the Son at Nicaea, and Cyril had defined the unity of the person of Christ at Ephesus, establishing that the divine and human natures meet in the one person of the eternal Son and that the woman who bore him bore not merely a man but God himself. Cyril's death on this day in the year four hundred and forty-four is the tradition's great departure feast of the tenth month: the patriarch who spent his episcopate in theological combat falls silent, and the silence that follows is the silence of a man who said everything that needed to be said and could now rest.

The theology of Cyril's departure in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the completed mission: Cyril did not die mid-task but finished. The Council of Ephesus had been convened, the heresy had been condemned, the formula 'Mother of God' had been secured for all subsequent Christian devotion, and the years between Ephesus and his death had allowed him to receive the vindication of his position and to see the Nestorian position reduced to schism rather than expanded to dominance. Cyril departs as a man who won — not because winning was his primary goal, but because the truth whose defence was his goal had prevailed, and the communities of the faithful who would gather for centuries after his death around the Theotokos in their liturgies would do so in part because Cyril had not yielded when yielding would have been far more comfortable than continuing to fight. His departure is the departure of one who finishes well, and the tradition's feast of his death is a celebration of that finishing.

Saint Methodius, Patriarch of Constantinople in the ninth century, enters this feast as a confessor of the second Iconoclast period: a man who suffered imprisonment and torture for his defence of the holy icons during the reign of the iconoclast emperors Leo V, Michael II, and Theophilos, and who was vindicated when the Empress Theodora restored the icons in eight hundred and forty-three in the great event known as the Triumph of Orthodoxy. Methodius was installed as Patriarch of Constantinople immediately after the restoration, and his brief patriarchate presided over the consolidation of the iconophile victory: the reintroduction of the icons into the liturgical life of Constantinople, the rehabilitation of the confessors who had suffered under the iconoclasts, and the establishment of the first Sunday of Lent as the Feast of Orthodoxy, the annual commemoration of the icon's restoration that remains in the Byzantine calendar to this day. Methodius is thus both a confessor and an architect of celebration: the man who suffered for the icons and who then organised the feast that would annually re-enact their triumph.

The monthly commemoration of Saint Jerome and the feast of Saint Vitus the Child-Martyr together seal the fourteenth day of Sene with two witnesses from opposite ends of the age spectrum and the intellectual register. Jerome of Bethlehem — whose monthly commemoration returns on this day — is the great scholar of the Western tradition, the translator of the Scriptures into the Latin Vulgate, the commentator of unmatched erudition, the controversialist of ferocious polemical energy, and the monk of Bethlehem who spent the last decades of his life in the cave near the site of the Nativity, surrounded by his books and his disciples and the women scholars led by Paula and Eustochium who had followed him from Rome. Vitus, by contrast, is a child: a Sicilian boy martyred under Diocletian, according to the tradition, at the age of twelve, along with his tutor Modestus and his nurse Crescentia, a child who had been converted by his Christian tutor and who was reported to his pagan father and then to the imperial authorities and who, in the manner of the tradition's child-martyrs, died before he had had time to sin much but not before he had had time to choose God. Jerome the aged scholar and Vitus the young martyr together frame the assembly of this rich fourteenth day: the one who thought his way to God across a lifetime of learning, and the child who arrived there before the learning had even begun. Amen.
